From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Reuse of sound recording in another recording

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/DJ_Premier_Digging33.jpg/250px-DJ_Premier_Digging33.jpg)](/wiki/File:DJ_Premier_Digging33.jpg)

[DJ Premier](/wiki/DJ_Premier "DJ Premier") selecting records to sample

In [sound](/wiki/Sound "Sound") and [music](/wiki/Music "Music"), **sampling** is the reuse of a portion (or **sample**) of a [sound recording](/wiki/Sound_recording "Sound recording") in another recording. Samples may comprise elements such as rhythm, melody, speech, or sound effects. A sample might comprise only a fragment of sound, or a longer portion of music, such as a drum beat or melody. Samples are often layered, [equalized](/wiki/Equalization_(audio) "Equalization (audio)"), sped up or slowed down, repitched, [looped](/wiki/Loop_(music) "Loop (music)"), or otherwise manipulated. They are usually integrated using electronic music instruments ([samplers](/wiki/Sampler_(musical_instrument) "Sampler (musical instrument)")) or software such as [digital audio workstations](/wiki/Digital_audio_workstation "Digital audio workstation").

A process similar to sampling originated in the 1940s with *[musique concrète](/wiki/Musique_concr%C3%A8te "Musique concrète")*, experimental music created by [splicing](/wiki/Tape_splice "Tape splice") and [looping tape](/wiki/Tape_loop "Tape loop"). The mid-20th century saw the introduction of keyboard instruments that played sounds recorded on tape, such as the [Mellotron](/wiki/Mellotron "Mellotron"). The term *sampling* was coined in the late 1970s by the creators of the [Fairlight CMI](/wiki/Fairlight_CMI "Fairlight CMI"), a [synthesizer](/wiki/Synthesizer "Synthesizer") with the ability to record and playback short sounds. As technology improved, cheaper standalone samplers with more [memory](/wiki/Computer_memory "Computer memory") emerged, such as the [E-mu Emulator](/wiki/E-mu_Emulator "E-mu Emulator"), [Akai S950](/wiki/Akai_S900 "Akai S900") and [Akai MPC](/wiki/Akai_MPC "Akai MPC").

Sampling is a foundation of [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop"), which emerged when producers in the 1980s began sampling [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk") and [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music") records, particularly [drum breaks](/wiki/Drum_break "Drum break"). It has influenced many other genres of music, particularly [electronic music](/wiki/Electronic_music "Electronic music") and [pop](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music"). Samples such as the [Amen break](/wiki/Amen_break "Amen break"), the "[Funky Drummer](/wiki/Funky_Drummer "Funky Drummer")" drum break and the [orchestra hit](/wiki/Orchestra_hit "Orchestra hit") have been used in thousands of recordings, and [James Brown](/wiki/James_Brown "James Brown"), [Loleatta Holloway](/wiki/Loleatta_Holloway "Loleatta Holloway"), [Fab Five Freddy](/wiki/Fab_Five_Freddy "Fab Five Freddy") and [Led Zeppelin](/wiki/Led_Zeppelin "Led Zeppelin") are among the most sampled artists. The first album created entirely from samples, *[Endtroducing](/wiki/Endtroducing..... "Endtroducing.....")* by [DJ Shadow](/wiki/DJ_Shadow "DJ Shadow"), was released in 1996.

Sampling without permission can [infringe copyright](/wiki/Copyright_infringement "Copyright infringement") or may be [fair use](/wiki/Fair_use "Fair use"). Clearance, the process of acquiring permission to use a sample, can be complex and costly; samples from well-known sources may be prohibitively expensive. Courts have taken different positions on whether sampling without permission is permitted. In *[Grand Upright Music, Ltd. v. Warner Bros. Records Inc](/wiki/Grand_Upright_Music,_Ltd._v._Warner_Bros._Records_Inc. "Grand Upright Music, Ltd. v. Warner Bros. Records Inc.")* (1991) and *[Bridgeport Music, Inc. v. Dimension Films](/wiki/Bridgeport_Music,_Inc._v._Dimension_Films "Bridgeport Music, Inc. v. Dimension Films")* (2005), American courts ruled that unlicensed sampling, however minimal, constitutes copyright infringement. However, *[VMG Salsoul v Ciccone](/wiki/VMG_Salsoul_v_Ciccone "VMG Salsoul v Ciccone")* (2016) found that unlicensed samples constituted *[de minimis](/wiki/De_minimis "De minimis")* copying, and did not infringe copyright. In 2019, the [European Court of Justice](/wiki/European_Court_of_Justice "European Court of Justice") ruled that modified, unrecognizable samples could be used without authorization. Though some artists sampled by others have complained of [plagiarism](/wiki/Plagiarism "Plagiarism") or lack of creativity, many commentators have argued that sampling is a creative act.

## Precursors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sampling_(music)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Precursors")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Phonogene.jpg/250px-Phonogene.jpg)](/wiki/File:Phonogene.jpg)

The Phonogene, a 1940s instrument which plays back sounds from tape loops

In the 1940s, the French composer [Pierre Schaeffer](/wiki/Pierre_Schaeffer "Pierre Schaeffer") developed *[musique concrète](/wiki/Musique_concr%C3%A8te "Musique concrète")*, an experimental form of music created by [recording sounds to tape](/wiki/Reel-to-reel_audio_tape_recording "Reel-to-reel audio tape recording"), splicing them, and manipulating them to create [sound collages](/wiki/Sound_collage "Sound collage"). He used sounds from the human body, locomotives, and kitchen utensils. The method also involved [tape loops](/wiki/Tape_loop "Tape loop"), splicing lengths of tape end to end so a sound could be played indefinitely. Schaeffer developed the [Phonogene](/wiki/Phonogene "Phonogene"), which played loops at 12 different pitches triggered by a keyboard.[[1]](#cite_note-Howell-2005-1)

Composers including [Pierre Henry](/wiki/Pierre_Henry "Pierre Henry"), [Karheinz Stockhausen](/wiki/Karlheinz_Stockhausen "Karlheinz Stockhausen"), [John Cage](/wiki/John_Cage "John Cage"), [Edgar Varèse](/wiki/Edgard_Var%C3%A8se "Edgard Varèse"), and [Iannis Xenakis](/wiki/Iannis_Xenakis "Iannis Xenakis") experimented with *musique concrète*. In the UK, it was brought to a mainstream audience by the [BBC Radiophonic Workshop](/wiki/BBC_Radiophonic_Workshop "BBC Radiophonic Workshop"), which used the techniques to produce soundtracks for shows including *[Doctor Who](/wiki/Doctor_Who "Doctor Who")* in the early 1960s.[[1]](#cite_note-Howell-2005-1)

In the 1960s, Jamaican [dub reggae](/wiki/Dub_music "Dub music") producers such as [King Tubby](/wiki/King_Tubby "King Tubby") and [Lee "Scratch" Perry](/wiki/Lee_%22Scratch%22_Perry "Lee \"Scratch\" Perry") began using recordings of [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae") rhythms to produce [riddim](/wiki/Riddim "Riddim") tracks, which were then [deejayed](/wiki/Deejay_(Jamaican) "Deejay (Jamaican)") over.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-mccann-3) Jamaican immigrants introduced the techniques to American [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop") in the 1970s.[[3]](#cite_note-mccann-3) [Holger Czukay](/wiki/Holger_Czukay "Holger Czukay") of the experimental German band [Can](/wiki/Can_(band) "Can (band)") spliced tape recordings into his music before the advent of digital sampling.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Techniques and tools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sampling_(music)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Techniques and tools")]

### Samplers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sampling_(music)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Samplers")]

Main article: [Sampler (musical instrument)](/wiki/Sampler_(musical_instrument) "Sampler (musical instrument)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Fairlight.JPG/250px-Fairlight.JPG)](/wiki/File:Fairlight.JPG)

The Fairlight CMI, a sampler and synthesizer released in 1979. The designers coined the term *sampling* to describe one of its features.

[The *Guardian*](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian") described the [Chamberlin](/wiki/Chamberlin "Chamberlin") as the first sampler, developed by the American engineer Harry Chamberlin in the 1940s. The Chamberlin used a keyboard to trigger a series of tape decks, each containing eight seconds of sound. Similar technology was popularised in the 60s with the [Mellotron](/wiki/Mellotron "Mellotron"). In 1969, the English engineer [Peter Zinovieff](/wiki/Peter_Zinovieff "Peter Zinovieff") developed the first digital sampler, the [EMS Musys](/wiki/EMS_Musys "EMS Musys").[[5]](#cite_note-McNamee-2009-5)

The term *sample* was coined by [Kim Ryrie](/wiki/Kim_Ryrie "Kim Ryrie") and [Peter Vogel](/wiki/Peter_Vogel_(computer_designer) "Peter Vogel (computer designer)") to describe a feature of their [Fairlight CMI](/wiki/Fairlight_CMI "Fairlight CMI") synthesizer, launched in 1979.[[1]](#cite_note-Howell-2005-1) While developing the Fairlight, Vogel recorded around a second of piano performance from a radio broadcast and discovered that he could imitate a piano by playing the recording back at different pitches. The result better resembled a real piano than sounds generated by synthesizers.[[6]](#cite_note-Newsscientist-6) Compared to later samplers, the Fairlight was limited; it allowed control over pitch and [envelope](/wiki/Envelope_(music) "Envelope (music)"), and could only record a few seconds of sound. However, the sampling function became its most popular feature. Though the concept of reusing recordings in other recordings was not new, the Fairlight's design and built-in [sequencer](/wiki/Music_sequencer "Music sequencer") simplified the process.[[1]](#cite_note-Howell-2005-1)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Akai_MPC60.jpg/250px-Akai_MPC60.jpg)](/wiki/File:Akai_MPC60.jpg)

The [Akai MPC](/wiki/Akai_MPC "Akai MPC"), an influential sampler produced from 1988

The Fairlight inspired competition, improving sampling technology and driving down prices. Early competitors included the [E-mu Emulator](/wiki/E-mu_Emulator "E-mu Emulator")[[1]](#cite_note-Howell-2005-1) and the [Akai S950](/wiki/Akai_S900 "Akai S900").[[7]](#cite_note-MusicRadar-7) [Drum machines](/wiki/Drum_machine "Drum machine") such as the [Oberheim DMX](/wiki/Oberheim_DMX "Oberheim DMX") and [Linn LM-1](/wiki/Linn_LM-1 "Linn LM-1") incorporated samples of drum kits and percussion rather than generating sounds from circuits.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Early samplers could store samples of only a few seconds in length, but this increased with improved [memory](/wiki/Computer_memory "Computer memory").[[9]](#cite_note-Milner-2011-9) In 1988, [Akai](/wiki/Akai_Professional "Akai Professional") released the first [MPC](/wiki/Akai_MPC "Akai MPC") sampler,[[10]](#cite_note-10) which allowed users to assign samples to pads and trigger them independently, similarly to playing a keyboard or drum kit.[[11]](#cite_note-Vox-11) It was followed by competing samplers from companies including [Korg](/wiki/Korg "Korg"), [Roland](/wiki/Roland_Corporation "Roland Corporation") and [Casio](/wiki/Casio "Casio").[[12]](#cite_note-M1-Retrozone-12)

Today, most samples are recorded and edited using [digital audio workstations](/wiki/Digital_audio_workstation "Digital audio workstation") (DAWs) such as [Pro Tools](/wiki/Pro_Tools "Pro Tools") and [Ableton Live](/wiki/Ableton_Live "Ableton Live").[[9]](#cite_note-Milner-2011-9)[[13]](#cite_note-McNamee-2008-13) As technology has improved, the possibilities for manipulation have grown.[[14]](#cite_note-Fact-2016-14)

### Sample libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sampling_(music)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Sample libraries")]

Samples are distributed in sample libraries, also known as sample packs. In the 1990s, sample libraries from companies such as [Zero-G](/wiki/Zero-G_Ltd "Zero-G Ltd") and [Spectrasonics](/wiki/Spectrasonics "Spectrasonics") were widely used in contemporary music.[[15]](#cite_note-10_classic-15) In the 2000s, [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") introduced "Jam Pack" sample libraries for its DAW [GarageBand](/wiki/GarageBand "GarageBand").[[16]](#cite_note-Music_2011-16) In the 2010s, producers began releasing sample packs on online platforms such as [Splice](/wiki/Splice_(platform) "Splice (platform)").[[17]](#cite_note-Tannenbaum_Tannenbaum_2021-17)

The Kingsway Music Library, created in 2015 by the American producer [Frank Dukes](/wiki/Frank_Dukes "Frank Dukes"),[[18]](#cite_note-18) has been used by artists including [Drake](/wiki/Drake_(musician) "Drake (musician)"), [Kanye West](/wiki/Kanye_West "Kanye West"), [Kendrick Lamar](/wiki/Kendrick_Lamar "Kendrick Lamar"), and [J. Cole](/wiki/J._Cole "J. Cole").[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) In 2020, the US [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress") created an [open-source](/wiki/Open-source "Open-source") web application that allows users to sample its library of copyright-free audio.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Interpolation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sampling_(music)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Interpolation")]

Main article: [Interpolation (popular music)](/wiki/Interpolation_(popular_music) "Interpolation (popular music)")

Instead of sampling, artists may recreate a recording, a process known as interpolation.[[22]](#cite_note-Jones-2022-22) This requires only the permission of the owners of the musical content, rather than the owners of the recording. It also creates more freedom to alter constituent components such as separate guitar and drum tracks.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## Impact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sampling_(music)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Impact")]

Sampling has influenced many genres of music,[[5]](#cite_note-McNamee-2009-5) particularly pop, hip-hop and electronic music.[[14]](#cite_note-Fact-2016-14) The *[Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* journalist David McNamee likened its importance in these genres to the importance of the guitar in rock.[[5]](#cite_note-McNamee-2009-5) In August 2022, the *Guardian* noted that half of the singles in the [UK Top 10](/wiki/UK_Top_10 "UK Top 10") that week used samples.[[22]](#cite_note-Jones-2022-22) Sampling is a fundamental element of [remix culture](/wiki/Remix_culture "Remix culture").[[24]](#cite_note-TechCrunch-24)

### Early works

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sampling_(music)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Early works")]

Using the Fairlight, the "first truly world-changing sampler", the English producer [Trevor Horn](/wiki/Trevor_Horn "Trevor Horn") became the "key architect" in incorporating sampling into pop music in the 1980s.[[5]](#cite_note-McNamee-2009-5) Other users of the Fairlight included [Kate Bush](/wiki/Kate_Bush "Kate Bush"), [Peter Gabriel](/wiki/Peter_Gabriel "Peter Gabriel") and [Thomas Dolby](/wiki/Thomas_Dolby "Thomas Dolby").[[7]](#cite_note-MusicRadar-7) In the 1980s, samples were incorporated into synthesizers and [music workstations](/wiki/Music_workstation "Music workstation"), such as the bestselling [Korg M1](/wiki/Korg_M1 "Korg M1"), released in 1988.[[12]](#cite_note-M1-Retrozone-12)

The [Akai MPC](/wiki/Akai_MPC "Akai MPC"), released in 1988, had a major influence on electronic and hip-hop music,[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[11]](#cite_note-Vox-11) allowing artists to create elaborate tracks without other instruments, a studio or formal music knowledge.[[11]](#cite_note-Vox-11) Its designer, [Roger Linn](/wiki/Roger_Linn "Roger Linn"), anticipated that users would sample short sounds, such as individual notes or drum hits, to use as building blocks for compositions; however, users sampled longer passages of music.[[9]](#cite_note-Milner-2011-9) In the words of Greg Milner, author of *Perfecting Sound Forever*, musicians "didn't just want the sound of [John Bonham's](/wiki/John_Bonham "John Bonham") kick drum, they wanted to loop and repeat the whole of '[When the Levee Breaks](/wiki/When_the_Levee_Breaks "When the Levee Breaks")'."[[9]](#cite_note-Milner-2011-9) Linn said: "It was a very pleasant surprise. After 60 years of recording, there are so many prerecorded examples to sample from. Why reinvent the wheel?"[[9]](#cite_note-Milner-2011-9)

[Stevie Wonder's](/wiki/Stevie_Wonder "Stevie Wonder") 1979 album [*Journey Through the Secret Life of Plants*](/wiki/Stevie_Wonder%27s_Journey_Through_%22The_Secret_Life_of_Plants%22 "Stevie Wonder's Journey Through \"The Secret Life of Plants\"") may have been the first album to make extensive use of samples.[[5]](#cite_note-McNamee-2009-5) The Japanese electronic band [Yellow Magic Orchestra](/wiki/Yellow_Magic_Orchestra "Yellow Magic Orchestra") were pioneers in sampling,[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-thevinyldistrict-27)[[28]](#cite_note-condry_59-28) constructing music by cutting fragments of sounds and [looping](/wiki/Looping_(music) "Looping (music)") them.[[28]](#cite_note-condry_59-28) Their album *[Technodelic](/wiki/Technodelic "Technodelic")* (1981) is an early example of an album consisting mostly of samples.[[27]](#cite_note-thevinyldistrict-27)[[29]](#cite_note-29) *[My Life in the Bush of Ghosts](/wiki/My_Life_in_the_Bush_of_Ghosts_(album) "My Life in the Bush of Ghosts (album)")* (1981) by [David Byrne](/wiki/David_Byrne "David Byrne") and [Brian Eno](/wiki/Brian_Eno "Brian Eno") is another important early work of sampling, incorporating samples of sources including [Arabic singers](/wiki/Arabic_music "Arabic music"), radio DJs and an [exorcist](/wiki/Exorcism "Exorcism").[[30]](#cite_note-30) Musicians had used similar techniques before, but, according to the *[Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* writer Dave Simpson, sampling had never before been used "to such cataclysmic effect".[[31]](#cite_note-Guardian-31) Eno felt the album's innovation was to make samples "the lead vocal".[[32]](#cite_note-Q-32) [Big Audio Dynamite](/wiki/Big_Audio_Dynamite "Big Audio Dynamite") pioneered sampling in rock and pop with their 1985 album *[This Is Big Audio Dynamite](/wiki/This_Is_Big_Audio_Dynamite "This Is Big Audio Dynamite")*.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Hip-hop

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sampling_(music)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Hip-hop")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/DJ_Shadow_tim_festival.jpg/250px-DJ_Shadow_tim_festival.jpg)](/wiki/File:DJ_Shadow_tim_festival.jpg)

[DJ Shadow](/wiki/DJ_Shadow "DJ Shadow")'s 1996 album *[Endtroducing](/wiki/Endtroducing..... "Endtroducing.....")* is cited as the first created entirely from samples.

Sampling is one of the foundations of [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop"), which emerged in the 1980s.[[34]](#cite_note-Fintoni-2013-34) Hip-hop sampling has been likened to the origins of [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues") and [rock](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music"), which were created by repurposing existing music.[[24]](#cite_note-TechCrunch-24) The *Guardian* journalist David McNamee wrote that "two record decks and your dad's old funk collection was once the working-class black answer to [punk](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock")".[[13]](#cite_note-McNamee-2008-13)

Before the rise of sampling, DJs [used turntables](/wiki/Turntablism "Turntablism") to loop [breaks](/wiki/Break_(music) "Break (music)") from records, which MCs would [rap](/wiki/Rapping "Rapping") over. Compilation albums such as *[Ultimate Breaks and Beats](/wiki/Ultimate_Breaks_and_Beats "Ultimate Breaks and Beats")* compiled tracks with drum breaks and solos intended for sampling, aimed at DJs and hip-hop producers*.*[[35]](#cite_note-The_Economist-35) In 1986, the tracks "[South Bronx](/wiki/South_Bronx_(song) "South Bronx (song)")", "[Eric B. is President](/wiki/Eric_B._is_President "Eric B. is President")" and "[It's a Demo](/wiki/It%27s_a_Demo "It's a Demo")" sampled the funk and soul tracks of [James Brown](/wiki/James_Brown "James Brown"), particularly a drum break from "[Funky Drummer](/wiki/Funky_Drummer "Funky Drummer")" (1970), helping popularize the technique.[[14]](#cite_note-Fact-2016-14)

The advent of affordable samplers such as the [Akai MPC](/wiki/Akai_MPC "Akai MPC") (1988) made looping easier.[[35]](#cite_note-The_Economist-35) *[Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records")* cites [DJ Shadow](/wiki/DJ_Shadow "DJ Shadow")'s acclaimed hip-hop album *[Endtroducing](/wiki/Endtroducing..... "Endtroducing.....")* (1996), made on an MPC60,[[36]](#cite_note-Taylor-36) as the first album created entirely from samples.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-Sullivan-38) The [E-mu SP-1200](/wiki/E-mu_SP-1200 "E-mu SP-1200"), released in 1987, had a ten-second sample length and a distinctive "gritty" sound, and was used extensively by [East Coast producers](/wiki/East_Coast_hip_hop "East Coast hip hop") during the [golden age of hip-hop](/wiki/Golden_age_of_hip-hop "Golden age of hip-hop") of the late 1980s and early 90s.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

### Common samples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sampling_(music)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Common samples")]

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg.png)

Commonly sampled elements include strings, basslines, drum loops, vocal [hooks](/wiki/Hook_(music) "Hook (music)") or entire bars of music, especially from [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music") records.[[40]](#cite_note-Salmon-2008-40) Samples may be layered, [equalized](/wiki/Equalization_(audio) "Equalization (audio)"),[[41]](#cite_note-The_Economist-2-41) sped up or slowed down, repitched, [looped](/wiki/Loop_(music) "Loop (music)") or otherwise manipulated.[[14]](#cite_note-Fact-2016-14)

A seven-second drum break in the 1969 track "Amen, Brother", known as the [Amen break](/wiki/Amen_break "Amen break"), became popular with American hip-hop producers and then British [jungle](/wiki/Jungle_music "Jungle music") producers in the early 1990s. It has been used in thousands of recordings, including songs by rock bands such as [Oasis](/wiki/Oasis_(band) "Oasis (band)") and theme tunes for television shows such as *[Futurama](/wiki/Futurama "Futurama")*, and is among the most sampled tracks in music history.[[35]](#cite_note-The_Economist-35) Other widely sampled drum breaks include the break from the 1970 [James Brown](/wiki/James_Brown "James Brown") song "[Funky Drummer](/wiki/Funky_Drummer "Funky Drummer")"; the [Think break](/wiki/Think_break "Think break"), sampled from the 1972 [Lyn Collins](/wiki/Lyn_Collins "Lyn Collins") song "[Think (About It)](/wiki/Think_(About_It) "Think (About It)")", written by Brown;[[42]](#cite_note-42) and the drum intro from [Led Zeppelin](/wiki/Led_Zeppelin "Led Zeppelin")'s 1971 song "[When the Levee Breaks](/wiki/When_the_Levee_Breaks "When the Levee Breaks")", played by [John Bonham](/wiki/John_Bonham "John Bonham") and sampled by artists including the [Beastie Boys](/wiki/Beastie_Boys "Beastie Boys"), [Dr. Dre](/wiki/Dr._Dre "Dr. Dre"), [Eminem](/wiki/Eminem "Eminem") and [Massive Attack](/wiki/Massive_Attack "Massive Attack").[[43]](#cite_note-43)

In 2014, the [*Smithsonian*](/wiki/Smithsonian_(magazine) "Smithsonian (magazine)") cited the most sampled track as "[Change the Beat](/wiki/Change_the_Beat "Change the Beat")" (1982) by [Fab Five Freddy](/wiki/Fab_Five_Freddy "Fab Five Freddy").[[44]](#cite_note-44) According to [WhoSampled](/wiki/WhoSampled "WhoSampled"), a user-edited website that catalogs samples, James Brown is sampled in more than 3,000 tracks, more than any other artist.[[45]](#cite_note-45) In 2011, *[The Independent](/wiki/The_Independent "The Independent")* named [Loleatta Holloway](/wiki/Loleatta_Holloway "Loleatta Holloway"), whose vocals were sampled in [house](/wiki/House_music "House music") and [dance](/wiki/Dance_music "Dance music") tracks such as "[Ride on Time](/wiki/Ride_on_Time "Ride on Time")" (1989) by [Black Box](/wiki/Black_Box_(band) "Black Box (band)"), as the most sampled female singer.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

The [orchestra hit](/wiki/Orchestra_hit "Orchestra hit") originated as a sound on the Fairlight, sampled from [Stravinsky's](/wiki/Igor_Stravinsky "Igor Stravinsky") 1910 orchestral work *[Firebird Suite](/wiki/Firebird_Suite "Firebird Suite"),*[[47]](#cite_note-Fink_2005_1-47): 1 and became a hip-hop cliché.[[48]](#cite_note-Fink_2005_6-48) *[MusicRadar](/wiki/MusicRadar "MusicRadar")* cited the *[Zero-G Datafiles](/wiki/Zero-G_Datafiles "Zero-G Datafiles")* sample libraries as a major influence on 90s dance music, becoming the "de facto source of breakbeats, bass and vocal samples".[[15]](#cite_note-10_classic-15)

## Legal and ethical issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sampling_(music)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Legal and ethical issues")]

To legally use a sample, an artist must acquire legal permission from the copyright holder, a potentially lengthy and complex process known as clearance. Sampling without permission can [breach the copyright](/wiki/Copyright_infringement "Copyright infringement") of the original sound recording, of the composition and lyrics, and of the performances, such as a rhythm or guitar riff. The [moral rights](/wiki/Moral_rights "Moral rights") of the original artist may also be breached if they are not credited or object to the sampling. In some cases, sampling is protected under American [fair use](/wiki/Fair_use "Fair use") laws,[[40]](#cite_note-Salmon-2008-40) which grant "limited use of copyrighted material without permission from the rights holder".[[49]](#cite_note-49) Deborah Mannis-Gardner of DMG Clearances, referring to the use of "[Somebody That I Used to Know](/wiki/Somebody_That_I_Used_to_Know "Somebody That I Used to Know")" in the [Doechii](/wiki/Doechii "Doechii") song "[Anxiety](/wiki/Anxiety_(Doechii_song) "Anxiety (Doechii song)")", said that the original recording was sampled, requiring consent from the [masters](/wiki/Mastering_(audio) "Mastering (audio)") rights holders rather than just the holders of publishing rights.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

The American musician [Richard Lewis Spencer](/wiki/Richard_Lewis_Spencer "Richard Lewis Spencer"), who owned the copyright for the widely sampled [Amen break](/wiki/Amen_break "Amen break"), never received [royalties](/wiki/Royalties "Royalties") for its use as the [statute of limitations](/wiki/Statute_of_limitations "Statute of limitations") for copyright infringement had passed by the time he learnt of the situation.[[51]](#cite_note-51) The journalist [Simon Reynolds](/wiki/Simon_Reynolds "Simon Reynolds") likened it to "the man who goes to the sperm bank and unknowingly sires hundreds of children".[[35]](#cite_note-The_Economist-35) [Clyde Stubblefield](/wiki/Clyde_Stubblefield "Clyde Stubblefield"), the performer of the widely sampled drum break from "[Funky Drummer](/wiki/Funky_Drummer "Funky Drummer")", also received no royalties.[[52]](#cite_note-:0-52) The owner of sampled material may not always be traceable, and such knowledge is commonly mislaid through corporate mergers, closures and buyouts.[[53]](#cite_note-NPR-2012-53)[[54]](#cite_note-Johns-54)

DJ Shadow said that artists tended to either see sampling as a mark of respect and a means to introduce their music to new audiences, or to be protective of their legacy and see no benefit.[[53]](#cite_note-NPR-2012-53) He described the difficulty of arranging compensation for each artist sampled in a work, and gave the example of two artists both demanding more than 50%, a mathematical impossibility. He instead advocated for a process of clearing samples on a [musicological](/wiki/Musicological "Musicological") basis, by identifying how much of the composition the sample comprises.[[55]](#cite_note-Convery-2017-55)

According to *Fact*, early hip-hop sampling was governed by "unspoken" rules forbidding the sampling of recent records, reissues, other hip-hop records or non-vinyl sources, among other restrictions. These rules were relaxed as younger producers took over and sampling became ubiquitous.[[34]](#cite_note-Fintoni-2013-34) In 2017, DJ Shadow said that he felt that "music has never been worth less as a commodity, and yet sampling has never been more risky".[[55]](#cite_note-Convery-2017-55)

Sampling can help popularize the sampled work. For example, the [Desiigner](/wiki/Desiigner "Desiigner") track "[Panda](/wiki/Panda_(Desiigner_song) "Panda (Desiigner song)")" (2015) reached number one on the *[Billboard Hot](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100")* [100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") after [Kanye West](/wiki/Kanye_West "Kanye West") sampled it on "[Father Stretch My Hands, Pt. 2](/wiki/Father_Stretch_My_Hands,_Pt._2 "Father Stretch My Hands, Pt. 2")" (2016).[[14]](#cite_note-Fact-2016-14) Some record labels and other [music licensing](/wiki/Music_licensing "Music licensing") companies have simplified their clearance processes by "pre-clearing" their records.[[56]](#cite_note-56) For example, the Los Angeles record label [Now-Again Records](/wiki/Now-Again_Records "Now-Again Records") has cleared songs produced for West and [Pusha T](/wiki/Pusha_T "Pusha T") in a matter of hours.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)

### Lawsuits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sampling_(music)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Lawsuits")]

In 1989, the [Turtles](/wiki/The_Turtles "The Turtles") sued [De La Soul](/wiki/De_La_Soul "De La Soul") for using an unlicensed sample on their album *[3 Feet High and Rising](/wiki/3_Feet_High_and_Rising "3 Feet High and Rising").* The Turtles singer, [Mark Volman](/wiki/Mark_Volman "Mark Volman"), told the *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")*: "Sampling is just a longer term for theft. Anybody who can honestly say sampling is some sort of creativity has never done anything creative." The case was settled out of court and set a [legal precedent](/wiki/Legal_precedent "Legal precedent") that had a [chilling effect](/wiki/Chilling_effect "Chilling effect") on sampling in hip-hop.[[59]](#cite_note-Runtagh-2016-59)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Biz_Markie_2016.jpg/250px-Biz_Markie_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Biz_Markie_2016.jpg)

[Biz Markie](/wiki/Biz_Markie "Biz Markie") in 2016

In 1991, the songwriter [Gilbert O'Sullivan](/wiki/Gilbert_O%27Sullivan "Gilbert O'Sullivan") sued the rapper [Biz Markie](/wiki/Biz_Markie "Biz Markie") after Markie sampled O'Sullivan's "[Alone Again (Naturally)](/wiki/Alone_Again_(Naturally) "Alone Again (Naturally)")" on the album *[I Need a Haircut](/wiki/I_Need_a_Haircut "I Need a Haircut")*. In *[Grand Upright Music, Ltd. v. Warner Bros. Records Inc](/wiki/Grand_Upright_Music,_Ltd._v._Warner_Bros._Records_Inc. "Grand Upright Music, Ltd. v. Warner Bros. Records Inc."),* the court ruled that sampling without permission infringed copyright. Instead of asking for royalties, O'Sullivan forced Markie's label, [Warner Bros](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Records "Warner Bros. Records"), to recall the album until the song was removed.[[60]](#cite_note-auto2-60)

The journalist [Dan Charnas](/wiki/Dan_Charnas "Dan Charnas") criticized the ruling, saying it was difficult to apply conventional copyright laws to sampling and that the American legal system did not have "the cultural capacity to understand this culture and how kids relate to it".[[61]](#cite_note-61) In 2005, the writer [Nelson George](/wiki/Nelson_George "Nelson George") described it as the "most damaging example of anti-hip-hop vindictiveness", which "sent a chill through the industry that is still felt".[[60]](#cite_note-auto2-60) In the *[Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")*, Chris Richards wrote in 2018 that no case had exerted more influence on pop music, likening it to banning a musical instrument. Some have accused the law of restricting creativity, while others argue that it forces producers to innovate.[[62]](#cite_note-Richards-62)

Since the O'Sullivan lawsuit, samples on commercial recordings have typically been taken either from obscure recordings or cleared, an often expensive option only available to successful acts.[[62]](#cite_note-Richards-62) According to the *Guardian*, "Sampling became risky business and a rich man's game, with record labels regularly checking if their musical property had been tea-leafed."[[13]](#cite_note-McNamee-2008-13) For less successful artists, the legal implications of using samples pose obstacles; according to *Fact*, "For a bedroom producer, clearing a sample can be nearly impossible, both financially and in terms of administration."[[14]](#cite_note-Fact-2016-14) By comparison, the 1989 [Beastie Boys](/wiki/Beastie_Boys "Beastie Boys") album *[Paul's Boutique](/wiki/Paul%27s_Boutique "Paul's Boutique")* is composed almost entirely of samples, most of which were cleared "easily and affordably"; the clearance process would be much more expensive today.[[63]](#cite_note-samples2-63) The *Washington Post* described the modern use of well known samples, such as on records by Kanye West, as an act of [conspicuous consumption](/wiki/Conspicuous_consumption "Conspicuous consumption") similar to flaunting cars or jewelry.[[62]](#cite_note-Richards-62) West has been sued several times over [his use of samples](/wiki/List_of_Kanye_West_samples_and_sampling_disputes "List of Kanye West samples and sampling disputes").[[14]](#cite_note-Fact-2016-14)

#### *De minimis* use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sampling_(music)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: De minimis use")]

In 2000, the jazz flautist [James Newton](/wiki/James_Newton "James Newton") filed a claim against the Beastie Boys' 1992 single "[Pass the Mic](/wiki/Pass_the_Mic "Pass the Mic")", which samples his composition "Choir". The judge found that the sample, comprising six seconds and three notes, was *[de minimis](/wiki/De_minimis "De minimis")* (small enough to be trivial) and did not require clearance. Newton lost appeals in 2003 and 2004.[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-Gardner-2016-65)

In the 2005 case *[Bridgeport Music, Inc. v. Dimension Films](/wiki/Bridgeport_Music,_Inc._v._Dimension_Films "Bridgeport Music, Inc. v. Dimension Films")*, the hip-hop group [N.W.A](/wiki/N.W.A "N.W.A"). were successfully sued for their use of a two-second sample of a [Funkadelic](/wiki/Funkadelic "Funkadelic") song in the 1990 track "[100 Miles and Runnin'](/wiki/100_Miles_and_Runnin%27_(song) "100 Miles and Runnin' (song)")". The [United States Court of Appeals for the Sixth Circuit](/wiki/United_States_Court_of_Appeals_for_the_Sixth_Circuit "United States Court of Appeals for the Sixth Circuit") ruled that all samples, no matter how short, required a license.[[14]](#cite_note-Fact-2016-14) A judge wrote: "Get a license or do not sample. We do not see this as stifling creativity in any significant way."[[65]](#cite_note-Gardner-2016-65)

As the Bridgeport judgement was decided in an American [circuit court](/wiki/United_States_courts_of_appeals "United States courts of appeals"), lower courts ruling on similar issues are bound to abide by it.[[14]](#cite_note-Fact-2016-14) However, in the 2016 case *[VMG Salsoul v Ciccone](/wiki/VMG_Salsoul_v_Ciccone "VMG Salsoul v Ciccone")*, the [United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit](/wiki/United_States_Court_of_Appeals_for_the_Ninth_Circuit "United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit") ruled that [Madonna](/wiki/Madonna "Madonna") did not require a license for a short horn sample in her 1990 song "[Vogue](/wiki/Vogue_(Madonna_song) "Vogue (Madonna song)")". The judge Susan Graber wrote that she did not see why sampling law should be an exception to standard *de minimis* law.[[65]](#cite_note-Gardner-2016-65)

In 2019, the [European Court of Justice](/wiki/European_Court_of_Justice "European Court of Justice") ruled that the producers [Moses Pelham](/wiki/Moses_Pelham "Moses Pelham") and Martin Haas had illegally sampled a drum sequence from the 1977 [Kraftwerk](/wiki/Kraftwerk "Kraftwerk") track "[Metal on Metal](/wiki/Metal_on_Metal_(song) "Metal on Metal (song)")" for the [Sabrina Setlur](/wiki/Sabrina_Setlur "Sabrina Setlur") song "Nur Mir". The court ruled that permission was required for recognizable samples; modified, unrecognizable samples could still be used without authorization.[[66]](#cite_note-BBC_News-2019-66)
